# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Deployment for our Node.js microservice. Focus on getting it deployed quickly - we'll optimize resource allocation later based on actual usage metrics. The service handles API requests and connects to a MongoDB database.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:   # metadata consists of the name, namespace (if any), labels etc for this deployment instance. 
    annotations: {}
spec:       # spec describes how to create and update instances in a Kubernetes cluster based on what we define here - such as replicas count or container images versions when updated if required by our business rules/configurations).
      selector:   # the Deployment uses this Selector for reconciliation.  We set up one ReplicaSet, which monitors how many pods are running and creates new ones (with replacement) until it's happy with 'replicas'. There should be a single Pod in each replica "template"
        matchLabels:   # This label is used to identify this deployment for selectors below. In our case its nodejs microservice will have that selector defined ‘app=node-js’ and the Deployment takes care of updating it when needed due requirement mentioned above about getting resources optimized quickly in later stage based on actual usage metrics
          app:    # Kind identifier used by Kubernetes for identifying which Pods to replace. It should match exactly with what is defined here ‘app=node-js’ (same as given at creation of this Deployment) and it must be unique within a namespace set in metadata section  
            name: node-js     # kind identifier used by Kubernetes for identifying the pods below, they are created inside our NodeJS microservice container. Must match exactly with 'app=node-js' ‘name’ defined above ('unique') and it must be unique within a namespace set in metadata section
      template:   # Defines how to create new Pod instances when requested by Kubernetes ReplicaSet, for the pods below this definition is used. We will define one Container where our NodeJS microservice code resides (run as well as MongoDB), and another that handles API requests 
        metadata:   # Defines additional attributes to add such as labels etc., if any are required by application logic/configurations, we can mention those here. It is optional in this scenario but would be necessary depending on the needs of your app or microservice instance. We will not set up a namespace for it
          annotations: {}  # In Kubernetes pods do have these defined such as environment variables if required by our application, that'll come here during Pod creation and then could change inside Container (if necessary) we need to mention them in this section. Optional though recommended especially when dealing with multi-tenancy
          labels:     # In Kubernetes pods are created based on these set of Selector(labels). So if there is a label like 'app=nodejs' defined above ('matchLabels'), then only that Pod which has the same Label('selector') will be taken care about this. This helps in ReplicaSet to know how many instances we should have and where our pod instance can live (placement)
            app: node-js    # Identifier used by Kubernetes for identifying with what kind of application these labels/labels are related or applied, must match exactly as 'app=node-js' here ‘name’ defined in Deployment. Optional if we want to reuse the same label definition across pods
      replicas:   # Number instance(Pod) you would like Kubernetes ReplicaSet (number of Pod instances that should be running at any time). In our case, it can have number between 1-3 where one is for MongoDB database and another two are used by the NodeJS microservice.
        spec:    # This section describes how pods will behave when they're created or updated based on what has been defined in this ReplicaSet specification (such as Docker Image versions, environment variables etc).  We could set up a MongoDB database Pod and another one used by NodeJS microservice which also must be mentioned here.
          selector:   # In Kubernetes pods are identified via Selectors(matchLabelKeys) defined in this section (must match exactly as we have declared above ie 'app=node-js'). So, when the ReplicaSet has a new created Pod and it is ready to serve requests from outside world, then that newly available NodeJS microservice pod instance will be used.
            matchLabels:   # The Kubernetes API selectors for this particular Deployment (defined in selector section). Must exactly matches with 'app=node-js' which we defined at the time of creation - a label must make up to what is set here, otherwise it can not work properly ‘matchlabels’
              app: node-js    # Identifier used by Kubernetes for identifying this pods (based on how many instance(pod) you want), that'll come inside 'Deployment'. It should match exactly as the definition at Deployments where we have defined our selector ie ‘app=node-js’
          containers:   # Container section in a Pod is used to contain an application and its dependencies. The pod itself (the whole system) does not move or stop until it has been told so, hence the container name 'MongoDB' for Mongodb database along with another one named as NodeJS which handles API requests
            -   # Single Container in a Pod can have multiple fields like image version etc. that’s why used '-'. This is basically what we call when creating/deploying an instance of this pod (Deployment). We will mention the Docker Image(version) and environment variable required by our NodeJS microservice here as well
              name: MongoDB   # Container 'name' in Kubernetes for referencifying where to find that particular container inside Pod. Must match exactly with ‘MongoDb’, which we defined when defining the deployment spec section ie MatchLabels below ('app=node-js') if required by our app logic or configurations
                image: mongo   # Docker Image(version) for 'mongodb' database in case that version is changed (update would require to change name as well). This cannot be updated later on unless we rollback. Optional depending upon whether this instance needs specific docker-image/tag and how it changes with updates
                env:    # Any environment variables set within the container need a definition here, if there's any then add them below (like 'MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME=username', etc). Also optional in this case as we have Docker image that includes all its dependencies already defined.
                  - MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME    # Environment Variable(must match exactly with our app logic or configurations, if any) to set when starting the container (run), for database authentication and password management purpose on startup of mongodb instance which must be available in Docker image.
                ports:   # Defines a list of Ports that will expose this Container within Pods - these can contain multiple Port definitions with different values if needed by our app logic or configurations, to make it possible for both communication between pod and the external world(DNS name service resolution) ‘port’.
                  - containerPort: 27017   # Specifies which port (number/name within Pods), this Container will expose in its network namespace where connection can be made to MongoDB database instance on host machine from any other pod or external world by using given Dns name when connected with the app. 'containerPort' must match exactly as we defined at deployment specification ie ‘27017’ and it should not vary if there is only one port that needs exposed in our case
                  - name: mongodb-port   # Defines a unique label(for Dns service resolution) for this Container within Pods. This will help when both 'app=nodejs' defined at Deployment (used to select the pod), and connection requirement between host machine/pod or external world, is provided from DNS server named in our deployment’
        strategy:   # Defines a rolling update behavior of deployments that include parameters such as maxSurge & minReadySeconds. We should not set up 'strategy' if we have only one pod to maintain for app instance(nodejs) - the rollout will take effect when it is available and there are no new updates scheduled 
          type: RollingUpdate   # Defines how rolling update behaviour, must be either of Rollback or Recreate (rolling update), i.e., if 'RollBack' then previous version can stay in place until all traffic has been redirected to the updated pods/version(pod). We have set up type as RollingUpdate so that rollout could work smoothly 
          rollingUpdate:   # Defines how much change before new Pod gets promoted into an active state and old one got removed. If there are changes in container images or env vars then update must be rolled out with the same amount of time to minimize disruption at app level (if any). This can vary from 1-30 seconds, default is '30s'
            maxUnavailable:   # Defines how many unhealthy replicas are allowed before a new replica gets scheduled. If there’re two nodes running old pod then they would be removed by Kubernetes to make room for the newer one(if not specified otherwise). ‘maxUnavailable'(allowed count), if set will ensure that at least 'specs' number of unhealthy instances are available (running in a cluster)
              staging:   # Defines how many old pod should be allowed before it starts removing new ones. If there’re two nodes running the newer one then they would not get removed until we allow them for all traffic to stabilize ie., if no data loss is expected, 'maxSurge' must match with this amount (if set in previous step) 
                staging: maxUnavailable    # Defines how many old pods should be allowed before the new ones start removing it. If there’re two nodes running and we need to ensure that all traffic has been redirected only when no data loss is expected then 'maxSurge' must match with this amount
